183 cm (6 ft) tall and now weighs 68 kg (150 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. His temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is
88/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 138/78 mm Hg. He is mentally alert. Physical examination
shows muscle fasciculations of the upper extremities and weakness of the lower extremities. The remainder of the
physical examination is most likely to show which of the following additional findings in this patient?
principal_context:
A 45-year-old man is brought to the clinic by his wife because of a 6-month history of progressive weakness; he also
has had dysphagia and a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight loss during this period. He has had to use a wheelchair during the past
3 weeks because of the weakness. He has no other history of major medical illness and takes no medications. He is a
former financial analyst and has no known toxin exposures. He does not smoke or drink alcoholic beverages. He is
183 cm (6 ft) tall and now weighs 68 kg (150 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. His temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is
88/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 138/78 mm Hg. He is mentally alert. Physical examination
shows muscle fasciculations of the upper extremities and weakness of the lower extremities. The remainder of the
physical examination is most likely to show which of the following additional findings in this patient?
information:
- ALS is a neurodegenerative disease that affects motor neurons, leading to progressive muscle weakness, atrophy, and fasciculations
- Absence of sensory loss makes ALS or IBM more likely
- ALS is characterized by progressive muscular weakness and atrophy, dysphagia, and fasciculations.
- Muscle atrophy might be revealed on physical examination.
raw_selection_response:
<reasoning>
The patient presents with a 6-month history of progressive weakness, dysphagia, weight loss, muscle fasciculations, and recent wheelchair dependency. The clinical profile is most suggestive of a progressive neurodegenerative disease affecting both upper and lower motor neurons. 

Key clues:
- Fasciculations suggest lower motor neuron involvement.
- Progressive weakness, atrophy, and dysphagia (bulbar involvement) point toward ALS or another motor neuron disease.
- Absence of sensory symptoms (e.g., loss of sensation/proprioception) supports a diagnosis focused on motor neurons rather than sensory neuropathy.
- Muscle atrophy and weakness are expected in ALS.
- Cranial nerve (bulbar) involvement (dysphagia) is common in ALS.
- Symptoms such as ptosis or visual changes are more suggestive of myasthenia gravis or other disorders, which are less likely given the progressive, symmetric pattern.
- Loss of deep tendon reflexes (areflexia) is more common in lower motor neuron syndromes like ALS late in the disease, but is not listed as a must in the question.
- Palmomental reflex is a sign of lower motor neuron disease and can be seen in ALS, but it is less emphasized clinically than atrophy or fasciculations in this context.
- Given the rapid progression and clinical context, atrophy is a nearly universal finding that would be seen on exam, alongside fasciculations and weakness.

Therefore, the most clinically relevant claims are those focused on ALS characteristics, expected physical findings, and differentiating features:
- Claim 1 and 8: ALS overview and classic clinical features (weakness, atrophy, fasciculations).
- Claim 3: Absence of sensory loss helps differentiate ALS from neuropathies.
- Claim 21: Atrophy as a likely physical exam finding given the clinical course.
  
Other claims (e.g., about genetic testing, ancillary tests, or management) are less relevant to the question of the "most likely additional physical examination finding" in this scenario.

</reasoning>
<answer>[1, 3, 8, 21]</answer>

agent_context:
A 25-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 6-day history of fever, severe muscle pain,
and diffuse, painful swelling of his neck, underarms, and groin area. The symptoms began after returning from a
camping trip in New Mexico. He appears ill and lethargic and can barely answer questions. His temperature is
39.2°C (102.5°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Physical
examination shows generalized scattered black maculae. Examination of the right upper extremity shows an
erythematous, solid, tender mass on the underside of the upper extremity just above the elbow; the mass is draining
blood and necrotic material. The most effective antibiotic for this patient’s disorder will interfere with which of the
following cellular processes or enzymes?
principal_context:
A 25-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 6-day history of fever, severe muscle pain,
and diffuse, painful swelling of his neck, underarms, and groin area. The symptoms began after returning from a
camping trip in New Mexico. He appears ill and lethargic and can barely answer questions. His temperature is
39.2°C (102.5°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Physical
examination shows generalized scattered black maculae. Examination of the right upper extremity shows an
erythematous, solid, tender mass on the underside of the upper extremity just above the elbow; the mass is draining
blood and necrotic material. The most effective antibiotic for this patient’s disorder will interfere with which of the
following cellular processes or enzymes?
information:
- The most effective antibiotic for Tick-borne Ehrlichiosis (TBE) is Doxycycline.
- Doxycycline is a tetracycline antibiotic that inhibits protein synthesis in bacteria by binding to the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome.
- Doxycycline interferes with protein synthesis by binding to the 30S subunit, preventing elongation of the peptide chain.
raw_selection_response:
<reasoning>
The patient's presentation—fever, muscle pain, generalized swollen lymph nodes, and a tender, necrotizing, erythematous mass after recent travel to New Mexico—strongly suggests a rickettsial infection acquired from a tick bite, such as Rocky Mountain spotted fever (RMSF), rather than bacterial cellulitis or other common bacterial causes. The presence of "black maculae" (eschar-like lesions) and necrotizing tissue further supports a severe, systemic rickettsial illness such as RMSF or, less likely, ehrlichiosis. 

The most effective antibiotic for RMSF and most rickettsial diseases is doxycycline, a tetracycline class antibiotic. Relevant claims include #1 ("The most effective antibiotic for Tick-borne Ehrlichiosis [or by extension, RMSF] is doxycycline") and #2/#3 (explaining that doxycycline inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding to the 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking elongation).

The question then asks which cellular process is targeted. Doxycycline is known to inhibit the bacterial ribosome's function, specifically binding to the 30S subunit and preventing tRNA binding and peptide chain elongation. Therefore, the relevant enzymatic or cellular process is ribosomal assembly/function. Among the answer options, "Ribosomal assembly" (E) is the best fit, as doxycycline inhibits the assembly and function of the ribosome by preventing translational elongation.

Claims #5 (regarding clindamycin) and #7 (#8, vancomycin and beta-lactams targeting cell wall synthesis) are less relevant, as these are alternative or less effective choices for rickettsial infections. Tetrahydrofolate reductase inhibitors (F) are used for antibiotics like trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole, which are not first-line here. The other options (B, C, D) do not correspond to the mechanism of action of doxycycline.

Thus, the most relevant supporting claims are #1 (identifying doxycycline as effective), #2 and #3 (mechanism of action—ribosomal inhibition).
</reasoning>
<answer>[1, 2, 3]</answer>

Thus, the most clinically relevant claims are those supporting the diagnosis of IE in this patient, the use of penicillin plus an aminoglycoside, and the mechanism by which aminoglycosides act (binding to the 30S ribosome to inhibit protein synthesis).

information:

- The most likely cause... is athletic-induced menstrual dysfunction (hypothalamic amenorrhea), potentially caused by her athletic activities.

agent_context:
A 6-day-old breast-fed boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother because of poor weight gain and
irritability since delivery, and a 2-hour history of vomiting. Physical examination shows jaundice and hepatomegaly.
A reducing substance test result of the urine is positive, and a glucose oxidase test result is negative. The
concentration of which of the following metabolites in liver is most likely increased in this patient?
principal_context:
A 6-day-old breast-fed boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother because of poor weight gain and
irritability since delivery, and a 2-hour history of vomiting. Physical examination shows jaundice and hepatomegaly.
A reducing substance test result of the urine is positive, and a glucose oxidase test result is negative. The
concentration of which of the following metabolites in liver is most likely increased in this patient?
information:
- Galactosemia leads to the accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate in the liver, which is toxic.
- The clinical presentation and lab findings (positive reducing substance, negative glucose oxidase) are consistent with galactosemia.
- The concentration of Galactose-1-phosphate in the liver is most likely increased in this patient.
raw_selection_response:
<reasoning>
To answer the clinical question, I need to connect the clinical presentation and test results to the underlying metabolic disorder, and then identify the metabolite that accumulates in the liver.

1. The patient is a 6-day-old breastfed boy with poor weight gain, irritability, jaundice, and hepatomegaly, and has been vomiting for 2 hours. This is suggestive of an inherited metabolic disorder of carbohydrate metabolism, as he is primarily on breast milk (contains lactose, which breaks down into glucose and galactose).
2. Laboratory findings:
   - Positive reducing substance test in urine: This suggests the presence of a reducing sugar other than glucose.
   - Negative glucose oxidase test: This rules out glucose as the reducing substance and supports that the reducing agent is another sugar like galactose.

3. Reviewing the claims:
   - Claim 3: Presentation and labs are consistent with galactosemia. **Relevant** – The clinical/lab findings describe classic galactosemia.
   - Claim 2: Galactosemia leads to accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate in the liver, which is toxic. **Relevant** – This identifies the key toxic metabolite.
   - Claim 8: The concentration of Galactose-1-phosphate in the liver is most likely increased. **Most directly answers the question**.
   - Claim 13: Galactosemia involves accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate in liver, leading to described clinical features. **Relevant** – Reinforces the pathophysiology.
   - Claim 14: Positive reducing substance test in galactosemia due to galactose in urine. **Relevant** – Explains the lab findings.
   - Claim 22: The positive reducing substance with negative glucose oxidase points to galactosemia. **Relevant** – Confirms the diagnostic reasoning.
   - Other claims about alternative diagnoses (biliary atresia, hemolytic anemia), lactose intolerance, and other disorders (HFI, CDG) are useful differentials but do not directly answer which metabolite is increased in this scenario.

4. Focusing on the metabolite:
   - Galactosemia → deficiency of galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase → accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate in liver → symptomatology.
   - Fructose-1-phosphate would be elevated in fructose intolerance (HFI), but the clinical/lab context points away from this.
   - The question directly asks which metabolite is increased in the liver, and it is galactose-1-phosphate.

Therefore, the most clinically relevant claims are those that (a) establish the diagnosis of galactosemia, (b) explain why the lab findings are as they are, and (c) specify which liver metabolite accumulates. These are claims 2, 3, 8, 13, and 22. For precision, the direct answer to the last part of the question is claim 8.

However, for full context and demonstration of clinical reasoning, including claims 2, 3, and 13 is also valuable.

Final selection rationale:
- Claim 2: Mechanism of metabolite accumulation.
- Claim 3: Links presentation and tests to galactosemia.
- Claim 8: Directly answers which metabolite is increased.
- Claim 13: Pathophysiology and clinical link.

But the **answer to the question** ("the concentration of which metabolite in liver is most likely increased?") is answer **B: Galactose 1-phosphate**, so claim 8 is the most directly relevant. However, since the question is about relevance to answering the scenario, claims 2, 3, and 8 are necessary to justify the choice. Claim 13 also supports this, but since claim 8 is explicit, it suffices.

I'll choose claims 2, 3, and 8 for a complete clinical reasoning pathway.
</reasoning>
<answer>[2, 3, 8]</answer>

principal_context:
A 54-year-old woman with a 40-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus comes to the office for a follow-up
examination. She is receiving hemodialysis for end-stage renal disease while awaiting a kidney transplant. As part of
her regimen, she also receives regular injections of a drug that induces reticulocyte release from the bone marrow
and stimulates a cytokine receptor that signals via the JAK/STAT pathway. This patient is most likely receiving
which of the following drugs?
information:
- The patient is currently receiving hemodialysis for end-stage renal disease.
- The patient receives regular injections of a drug that induces reticulocyte release and stimulates the JAK/STAT pathway.
- The description of the medication's mechanism of action is consistent with erythropoiesis-stimulating therapy (EST), specifically erythropoietin (EPO) or epoetin alfa.
- EST is a common treatment for anemia in patients with chronic kidney disease, including those receiving hemodialysis.
- The use of EST is a well-established treatment for anemia in patients with chronic kidney disease, including those receiving hemodialysis.
- The JAK/STAT pathway is a critical signaling pathway involved in erythropoiesis.
- The induction of reticulocyte release is a key action of erythropoietin.
- ESAs do interact with the JAK/STAT pathway to some extent via erythropoietin receptor activation.
- The patient is most likely receiving an erythropoiesis-stimulating agent such as Epoetin alfa or Darbepoetin alfa for managing anemia associated with her ESRD and hemodialysis.
- These medications are standard of care for anemia management in patients with CKD on dialysis.
raw_selection_response:
<reasoning>
The key aspects of the clinical question are:

- The patient has end-stage renal disease (ESRD) on hemodialysis, which commonly leads to anemia due to erythropoietin deficiency.
- The drug in question induces reticulocyte release and acts via the JAK/STAT pathway.
- We need to identify which of the given drugs fits this mechanism and is used in this clinical context.

Relevant claims analysis:
- **Claim 2**: Patient is on hemodialysis for ESRD—this sets the context for anemia management.
- **Claim 4**: The drug induces reticulocyte release and stimulates the JAK/STAT pathway—this directly matches the mechanism described in the question.
- **Claim 5**: The medication profile matches erythropoiesis-stimulating therapy (EST) like erythropoietin (EPO) or epoetin alfa—this narrows the drug class based on mechanism and use.
- **Claim 6 and 9**: EST is standard treatment for anemia in ESRD/hemodialysis patients—confirms appropriateness of the therapy.
- **Claim 11**: Erythropoietin induces reticulocyte release—fits the described drug action.
- **Claim 16 and 17**: ESAs such as Epoetin alfa or Darbepoetin alfa are standard for anemic ESRD/hemodialysis patients.
- **Claim 15 and 10**: JAK/STAT pathway is involved in the action of these agents via the erythropoietin receptor.
- Other claims about alternative agents (e.g., peginesatide, sargramostim [GM-CSF], interleukin-2, platelet-derived growth factor) can be eliminated based on the mechanism and clinical context:
    - **Filgrastim and Sargramostim (Options B and E)** act on granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF), increasing neutrophils, not inducing reticulocyte release.
    - **Interleukin-2 (Option C)** is involved in T-cell activation, not erythropoiesis.
    - **Platelet-derived growth factor (Option D)** stimulates platelet and connective tissue growth, not erythropoiesis.

Combining these, the most relevant claims for narrowing down the correct answer are those that describe the clinical setting, the mechanism of action, and the standard therapy for anemia in ESRD patients on hemodialysis. These clearly point to erythropoietin (or an ESA like epoetin alfa).
</reasoning>
<answer>[2, 4, 5, 6, 9, 11, 16, 17, 10, 15]</answer>

cervix and anterior wall of the vagina show well-differentiated keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma. Which of the
following best describes the pathogenesis of this patient's disease?
information:
- Cervical cancer is primarily caused by persistent infection with high-risk types of human papillomavirus (HPV), most commonly types 16 and 18.
- Persistent HPV infection leads to the activation of cellular oncogenes and the inactivation of tumor suppressor genes (e.g., p53 and Rb), promoting cellular proliferation and evasion of apoptosis.
- The transformation process involves initial infection, establishment of persistent infection, genetic alterations, epithelial dysplasia, and progression to invasive cancer.
raw_selection_response:

To answer the question about the pathogenesis of this patient's cervical squamous cell carcinoma, the most critical claim is the one that describes the underlying mechanism leading to the development of this cancer. The question specifically asks about pathogenesis, not clinical findings, staging, or treatment.

The pathology section of the case mentions well-differentiated keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma confirmed by biopsy. In terms of pathogenesis, the most internationally recognized cause of cervical cancer is infection with high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV), which leads to genetic alterations in host cells. Claim 15 states:
"15. Cervical cancer is primarily caused by persistent infection with high-risk types of human papillomavirus (HPV), most commonly types 16 and 18. These viruses integrate into the host DNA, leading to the transformation of cervical epithelial cells to a malignant phenotype..."

This claim addresses the fundamental cause and early steps in the pathogenesis (HPV infection leading to genetic transformation). Additional supporting claims 16, 17, and 18 elaborate on how HPV leads to activation of oncogenes (like c-MYC or transactivation events via viral proteins such as E6/E7 leading to inactivation of tumor suppressors like p53 and Rb), which are molecular mechanisms relevant to cancer development.

Among the answer options:

- B, C, D — These options relate more to other tumor processes (e.g., viral insertional mutagenesis, transduction, or HTLV-1/TAX in adult T-cell leukemia).
- E. Translocation of c-MYC to an Ig gene promoter — This is the pathogenesis seen in certain B-cell lymphomas (e.g., Burkitt’s lymphoma), not cervical carcinoma.

Given this, the most clinically relevant claims for pathogenesis are 15, 16, 17, and 18. These best describe the sequence from HPV infection to malignant transformation, aligning with both the clinical scenario and answer option A (inactivation of p53) as a downstream event, but the primary pathogenic process is HPV-mediated carcinogenesis as described in claims 15–18.

However, since the question specifically asks for the pathogenesis "best describing" the disease and the answer options include the inactivation of p53 (A), claim 17 (which notes inactivation of tumor suppressor genes like p53) is also clinically relevant. But the initiating factor is HPV infection, so claim 15 is foundational.

Therefore, the most relevant claims are:
- 15 (HPV as cause and initial transformation)
- 17 (p53 inactivation as a key mechanistic step)
- 18 (multi-step transformation pathway)

Claims about clinical management or staging (e.g., claims 1–14, 19–34) are not relevant to the pathogenesis question.

</reasoning>
<answer>[15, 17, 18]</answer>

of a killed vaccine relative to a live vaccine is the most appropriate rationale for developing a killed vaccine for this
illness?
principal_context:
A new severe respiratory illness caused by a newly identified virus is discovered. Which of the following properties
of a killed vaccine relative to a live vaccine is the most appropriate rationale for developing a killed vaccine for this
illness?
information:
- Killed vaccines have no potential for reversion to virulence.
- Killed vaccines are generally safer than live vaccines (no risk of reversion, no risk of transmission, and milder side effects).
- The ability to safely vaccinate a broad range of individuals, including those who cannot receive live vaccines, is a key rationale.
raw_selection_response:
<reasoning>
The clinical question focuses on the most appropriate rationale for choosing a killed vaccine over a live vaccine for a new, severe respiratory illness with a high safety concern profile. The core considerations are safety, risk mitigation (especially in immunocompromised populations), and practical deployment.

Let’s analyze the options:

A. Avoids the concern for reversion to virulence  
This directly addresses a major risk of live vaccines, which can mutate back into a virulent form, though rare. Both claims 6, 7, 10, and 21 highlight the safety benefit due to absence of reversion risk with killed vaccines.

B. Develops more rapid protective immunity  
This is generally not true, and claim 12 notes killed vaccines induce less robust and shorter-lasting immunity. So, claims supporting a stronger or faster immune response are more relevant to live vaccines (claims 9), not killed vaccines.

C. Is less likely to require subsequent boosters for lifelong immunity  
Killed vaccines often require more frequent boosters because immunity wanes faster (see claim 12). So this claim does not support using a killed vaccine as the best option compared to live vaccines.

D. Is most likely to generate mucosal immunity  
Live vaccines are more likely to stimulate mucosal immunity due to replication at the site of infection. Killed vaccines are less likely to do so (no direct claim, but see claim 14 and 12 about less robust response). So this is not a rationale for choosing killed over live.

E. Requires little safety monitoring to ensure inactivation  
Actually, producing killed vaccines requires rigorous inactivation and safety monitoring (contrary to this option), so it is not a good rationale (claim 3 and 16 say killed vaccines are safer but not that they need less safety monitoring). 

Now, the most clinically relevant rationale is safety—specifically, avoiding risk of reversion to virulence and avoiding risk of infecting immunocompromised contacts or people with contraindications. Claims supporting killed vaccines’ safety—especially the absence of risk of reversion or transmission—are critical:

Relevant claims:
- 6: Killed vaccines have no potential for reversion to virulence.
- 10: Live vaccines carry risks such as reversion to virulence... 
- 11: Killed vaccines are generally safer... no risk of reversion, no risk of transmission, and milder side effects.
- 15: The ability to safely vaccinate a broad range of individuals, including those who cannot receive live vaccines, is a key rationale.
- 16: Killed vaccines are generally considered safer than live vaccines because they cannot cause the disease...
- 21: The most appropriate property... is safety.

Of these, the strongest, evidence-based, and exam-relevant claims are 6, 11, 15, 16, and 21. However, the most direct, concise, and commonly emphasized rationale in clinical settings for choosing a killed vaccine over live in a novel, severe illness is the avoidance of virulence reversion and broad safety for all populations (including immunocompromised).

Thus, the most relevant claims are:
- 6 (no risk of reversion to virulence)
- 11 (killed vaccines are safer, no risk of reversion/transmission)
- 15 (can safely vaccinate broader population, including those who cannot take live vaccines)
- 21 (safety is the most appropriate property)

However, since the question asks for the *most appropriate rationale*, the avoidance of reversion to virulence stands out as the primary and unique risk of live vaccines. Therefore, claims 6 and 11 are paramount. Claim 15 is also important due to the patient population, but the question's context (new severe illness) emphasizes acute safety.

Best selection for focused, exam-style answers would be claims about **averting reversion and safety for all patients**:

**Final selection based on strongest evidence and clarity:**
- 6: Killed vaccines have no potential for reversion to virulence.
- 11: Killed vaccines are generally safer than live vaccines (no risk of reversion, no risk of transmission...).
- 15: The ability to safely vaccinate a broad range of individuals, including those who cannot receive live vaccines, is a key rationale.

Option A is the best answer among the choices, and these claims directly support that rationale.

</reasoning>
<answer>[6, 11, 15]</answer>

during a 15-minute session on a treadmill. The average pulse for the last 2 minutes of the session is 175/min. During

which of the following is most likely to be decreased?
principal_context:
During a clinical study examining the effects of exercise, men between the ages of 20 and 30 years are evaluated
during a 15-minute session on a treadmill. The average pulse for the last 2 minutes of the session is 175/min. During
the last minute of exercise, various measurements are taken. Compared with the measurement before the session,
which of the following is most likely to be decreased?
information:
- Exercise typically causes an increase in BP, but the magnitude of the increase can vary.
- After cessation of intense exercise, heart rate is expected to drop significantly as the body transitions from anaerobic to aerobic state.
raw_selection_response:
<reasoning>
To answer the question, we need to focus on the physiological changes that occur at the end of an exercise session, particularly during the post-exercise recovery period. Specifically, we are asked what is MOST LIKELY to be DECREASED compared to baseline after intense exercise in this age group.

Key points from the question:
- The heart rate remains elevated at 175/min during the last 2 minutes (intense phase).
- We are considering measurements taken at the end of exercise or immediately after (i.e., during recovery).
- We need to identify which parameter is most likely to decrease from its pre-exercise value.

Now, let's evaluate each option in light of the provided claims:

A. **Cardiac Output**: Usually increases during exercise due to both increased heart rate and stroke volume, and may remain elevated or begin to decrease post-exercise but not immediately.
  
B. **Oxygen Consumption**: Increases during exercise and remains elevated or only begins to decrease minutes after stopping; not the first to decrease.
  
C. **Pulse Pressure**: This is the difference between systolic and diastolic blood pressure. During exercise, systolic BP often increases significantly, while diastolic BP may remain unchanged or even decrease due to vasodilation in non-exercising vascular beds. Thus, pulse pressure usually increases during exercise and would not be the first to decrease post-exercise.
  
D. **Stroke Volume**: Increases during exercise due to increased preload and contractility, and may decrease slightly post-exercise as heart rate drops and systemic demand reduces, but is not the most immediate or dramatic decrease.
  
E. **Systolic Blood Pressure**: During exercise, systolic BP typically increases. However, after stopping intense exercise, it tends to decrease below baseline due to sudden reduction in muscle blood flow, venous return, and sympathetic withdrawal.
  
F. **Total Peripheral Resistance (TPR)**: TPR decreases during exercise due to active vasodilation in working muscles. Post-exercise, as blood flow redistributes, TPR may actually revert back to or even drop below baseline briefly before stabilizing, but the most striking and classic post-exercise change is a fall in systolic BP.

From the claims, the most relevant are:
- #5 (“Exercise typically causes an increase in BP, but the magnitude of the increase can vary.”) — helps us understand that BP responds to exercise and can change post-exercise.
- #20 (“After cessation of intense exercise, heart rate is expected to drop significantly as the body transitions from anaerobic to aerobic state.”) — highlights that major cardiovascular variables return toward baseline after stopping.
- #8 (“Exercise can cause shifts in vascular resistance due to vasodilation...”) — informs that TPR falls during exercise but may rebound post-exercise.
But the most critical claim is:
- The general physiological knowledge (which is echoed in #10 and #20) that systolic blood pressure typically falls below baseline immediately after stopping intense exercise due to reduced central blood volume and vasodilation.

Thus, **E. Systolic blood pressure** is most likely to be decreased after exercise when compared to the pre-exercise value. The heart rate (while high during the last minute) will start to drop soon after stopping (claims #10, #12, #20), but the question asks which measurement taken "during the last minute of exercise" (but compared to pre-session) is most likely to be decreased. However, since most hemodynamic values immediately after stopping drop, systolic BP is the classic parameter that can go below baseline right after exercise ends.

Relevant claims:
- **#5**: Explains BP rises during exercise but may vary—implies post-exercise BP can fall below baseline.
- **#20**: Heart rate drops after exercise, but the question's options do not include HR directly.
- **#8**: TPR decreases during exercise, but this may normalize or even overcorrect post-exercise.
- The most direct inference for a decreased measurement is **systolic blood pressure**, supported by #5 and knowledge from #20 about post-exercise transition.

Therefore, the most clinically relevant claims are:
1. Claim 5 (BP changes during and after exercise)
2. Claim 20 (post-exercise hemodynamic transition leading to decreased HR and BP)

But since the question specifically asks which parameter decreases, and systolic BP is the best answer, we select the claims that directly support this physiological response.

Final selection is **claim 5 and claim 20**. However, **claim 20** refers to HR, not BP—but its point about post-exercise transition is still relevant. Since the decrease in BP is more directly addressed by claim 5 and the general transition described by claim 20, these are the best matches.

But narrowing down:
- The strongest direct support for decreased systolic BP is in recognizing that after stopping exercise, a fall below baseline is common and is supported by claim 5 and the physiological reasoning in #20.

However, given the answer choices, the most specific supporting claim is **#5** (BP changes during and after exercise), plus **#20** gives context about post-exercise hemodynamic changes.

Given the options, the **most clinically relevant are claim 5 and claim 20**, but since systolic BP is the answer, **claim 5** is the most directly relevant.

But let's also review the other parameters for completeness:
- **Cardiac output**: Likely not decreased yet (may still be high or starting to drop).
- **Oxygen consumption**: Still elevated.
- **Pulse pressure**: Usually higher during exercise.
- **Stroke volume**: May drop, but not as immediately or sharply as BP.
- **TPR**: Usually returns to baseline or slightly below after exercise, but BP drop is more dramatic.

Therefore, the best-supported answer is **E. Systolic blood pressure**, supported by **claim 5**.

However, to be thorough and reference post-exercise hemodynamics, including **claim 20** adds value.

**Final relevant claims:**  
- #5 (“Exercise typically causes an increase in BP, but the magnitude of the increase can vary.”)  
- #20 (“After cessation of intense exercise, heart rate is expected to drop significantly as the body transitions from anaerobic to aerobic state.”) — this supports the concept of post-exercise hemodynamic transition, which includes BP falling below baseline.

But since the question specifically asks for a *decrease* compared to baseline and not just a change, and because systolic BP frequently drops below baseline post-exercise, **#5 and #20 are most clinically relevant**.

If only one is allowed, **#5** is the single best direct support. But per the format, we can include both.

---

</reasoning>
<answer>[5, 20]</answer>

information:

- Consider adjusting medication regimen, including dose and frequency, to achieve more consistent asthma control.
- Nightly coughing once a week suggests possible nocturnal asthma.
- The primary concern remains asthma control, which might not be optimal despite infrequent albuterol use.
- Daily controller medications like inhaled corticosteroids or long-acting beta agonists should be considered based on her history and symptoms.
- Environmental triggers like gas heaters should be discussed and exposure reduced.
- The most appropriate next step is to review and adjust the asthma management plan, which includes adding a daily controller medication.

The patient is a 9-year-old girl with a 4-year history of asthma who uses inhaled albuterol as needed, but now has nighttime cough episodes weekly and a sedentary lifestyle due to cough when active. Key issues:
1. **Suboptimal asthma control:** Nighttime cough, activity limitation, reliance on as-needed albuterol, and decreased quality of life indicate the current regimen is insufficient.
2. **Lifestyle and obesity:** Her sedentary lifestyle and overweight status may worsen asthma and complicate control.
3. **Need for controller therapy:** Current guidelines recommend regular controller medications (e.g., inhaled corticosteroids) for persistent asthma, even if symptoms seem mild or patients use albuterol only occasionally.
4. **Assessment before medication escalation:** While spirometry is helpful, in a clear-cut case of persistent asthma symptoms and risk factors, starting a controller is often warranted for children.
5. **Education and trigger modification:** Addressing environmental and lifestyle factors is important but not the immediate next step for medication management.
6. **Exclusion of other diagnoses:** Given the history of asthma, the clinical context, and physical findings, other causes (e.g., chronic bronchitis, vocal cord dysfunction) are possible but less likely, especially given clear lungs and known asthma history.

Most clinically relevant claims:
- **1, 5, 8, 11, 22, 25, 28**

11. *The primary concern remains asthma control, which might not be optimal despite infrequent albuterol use.* (Reinforces need for change.)
22. *Daily controller medications... should be considered based on her history and symptoms.* (Guideline-based recommendation.)

28. *The most appropriate next step is to review and adjust the asthma management plan, which includes adding a daily controller medication.* (Directly supports the correct answer, which is to add a daily controller such as fluticasone.)

Options such as adding oral theophylline (A) or short-term oral steroids (D) are not generally first-line. - Parkinson's disease can cause postural instability and gait difficulties due to postural instability, which can lead to falls, especially backward.
- Dopaminergic therapy can cause dyskinesias that might exacerbate falls.
- One treatment option is to review and optimize the medication regimen, including decreasing or modifying dopaminergic medications if contributing to postural instability.
- Implement physical therapy interventions including balance/gait training, assistive devices, and strength/flexibility training.

The classic differential for these findings in a child includes **nephrotic syndrome** and **post-infectious glomerulonephritis (PIGN)**. Let's analyze the options using the available claims.

### Option Analysis Using Relevant Claims

#### A. **Hemoglobin**
- **Claim 16:** CBC can look for anemia, which can be seen in chronic kidney disease.
- In acute nephrotic syndrome or PIGN, anemia may not be present early. However, chronicity or underlying disease could cause it, but it's not the **most likely** decrease in *this* scenario.

#### B. **Serum albumin concentration**
- **Claim 3, 13, 14, 35:** Albumin levels are expected to be decreased in nephrotic syndrome due to massive urinary protein loss, leading to reduced oncotic pressure and edema.

- **Claim 2, 29, 36:** Decreased in systemic autoimmune diseases like SLE, not typical of acute nephrotic syndrome or PIGN.

#### D. **Serum triglycerides concentration**
- Not addressed in the provided claims, but generally, nephrotic syndrome can cause hyperlipidemia (increased triglycerides), not a decrease.
- Even if not directly mentioned, triglycerides are not expected to decrease.

#### E. **Serum urea nitrogen (BUN) concentration**
- **Claim 15, 26:** BUN is part of the BMP to assess renal function, but in early nephrotic syndrome or PIGN, BUN may be **normal or only mildly elevated** since the glomerular filtration rate is often preserved in children.
- Decreased BUN is not a typical finding unless there is specific prerenal azotemia (e.g., dehydration), which is not the main cause here.

has type 2 diabetes mellitus. Current medications are metformin and pantoprazole. She reports loose stools four times daily during 
the past 2 weeks. In addition to wearing a clean isolation gown, which of the following is the most appropriate precaution for the 
physician to take?
information:
- VRE can be transmitted to healthcare workers
- Physician should follow strict infection control practices to minimize risk of transmitting pathogens including VRE
- Wearing a clean isolation gown by the physician is prudent.
- Good hygiene practices and contact precautions are reasonable until confirmed resolution of diarrhea.
- C. difficile recurrence is the most likely diagnosis given the history of vancomycin use and persistent symptoms.
- CDC guidelines recommend contact precautions for patients with C. difficile, including wearing gloves and gowns.
- Contact precautions are necessary to prevent spread of the pathogen to healthcare personnel and other patients.
- The most plausible explanation for her ongoing symptoms is a recurrence of C. difficile infection.
- Contact precautions are the most appropriate measure to prevent spread of C. difficile within healthcare facilities.
raw_selection_response:
<reasoning>
The patient has recently received oral vancomycin for Clostridioides difficile colitis and is now presenting with persistent loose stools, which raises concern for recurrence of C. difficile infection. According to CDC guidelines, patients with active or suspected C. difficile infection should be placed on contact precautions to prevent transmission to healthcare workers and other patients. 

The use of oral vancomycin increases the risk of colonization or transmission of vancomycin-resistant enterococci (VRE) as well (claim 1 and 2). However, the most immediate and relevant precaution for the setting of ongoing diarrhea post-vancomycin therapy is addressing the risk of C. difficile transmission. 

Strict infection control, including the use of gloves and gowns along with isolation precautions, is advised (claims 3, 11, 19, 20, 22). Washing hands thoroughly with soap and water is preferred over alcohol-based hand rub for C. difficile because spores are less susceptible to alcohol (claim 12 and standard practices). Wearing a surgical mask (C) is not specifically indicated for C. difficile, and preparing the staple site with chlorhexidine and wearing sterile gloves (D) is unnecessary for preventing pathogen transmission in this clinical scenario. No additional precautions are not appropriate because ongoing diarrhea after treatment for C. diff is suggestive of recurrence (claims 15, 21).

Thus, the most clinically relevant claims are those that underline the need for contact precautions due to likely ongoing C. difficile infection, the prior vancomycin use increasing pathogen-related risk, and the proper hand hygiene technique for this context.
</reasoning>
<answer>[2, 3, 11, 12, 15, 19, 20, 21, 22]</answer>

information:
- PCOS is characterized by hyperandrogenism, but the presence of an ovarian mass makes this diagnosis less likely.
- The differential diagnosis for a single ovarian mass includes benign tumors (cystadenoma, dermoid cyst), borderline tumors, malignant tumors (carcinoma), and functional tumors.
- Biopsy of the ovarian mass via laparoscopy is often required for definitive diagnosis.
- Normal androstenedione, DHEA, and 17-ketosteroids indicate the excess androgen is not from an adrenal source.
- A 12-cm ovarian mass is suggestive of an ovarian neoplasm.

- Additionally, surgical biopsy/definitive diagnosis methods (claim 8) are relevant for next steps but not for naming the mass.

Ovarian thecoma (E) produces estrogen but not typically testosterone. Granulosa tumors (A) can produce estrogen ± testosterone but are less associated with virilization in premenopausal women than Sertoli-Leydig tumors. Carcinoid and teratoma tumors do not typically present this way (B and D are less likely). Thus, from the symptom and lab profile, and ruling out other etiologies (claims 2, 4, 15, 16), the best answer is C. Sertoli-Leydig tumor.

But since the assistant must pick from the listed claims that support the reasoning, not the answer itself, the most relevant claims are:

agent_context:
The abstract displayed on this page is for use with items #17–19 on the following page. 
Question 
In patients with opioid use disorder, what is the relative efficacy of buprenorphine and methadone maintenance therapy? 
Review scope 
Included studies compared buprenorphine maintenance therapy (BMT), >1 mg/day, with methadone maintenance therapy (MMT), 
≥20 mg/day, or placebo (including 1 mg/day of buprenorphine) in patients who were dependent on heroin or other opioids. Studies of 
pregnant women and those assessing buprenorphine or methadone for detoxification but with no maintenance phase were excluded. 
Outcomes included treatment retention and urinalysis-confirmed use of morphine, cocaine, and benzodiazepines. 
Review methods 
MEDLINE and EMBASE/Excerpta Medica (2003 to Jan 2013), Cochrane Central and Cochrane Library (2013, Issue 1), PsycLIT, Current Contents, several other databases, 
ClinicalTrials.gov  and other electronic sources of ongoing trials, conference proceedings, 
Library of Congress databases, national focal points for drug research, and reference lists were searched for randomized controlled 
trials (RCTs). Authors were consulted. 31 RCTs (n=5430), ranging in size from 40 to 736 patients, met the selection criteria. Duration of study interventions ranged from 2 weeks to 52 weeks. 20 trials compared BMT with MMT, and 11 compared BMT with placebo. 
Main results 
As shown in the table below, low-, medium-, and high-dose BMT increased treatment retention more than placebo; BMT did not 
differ from equivalent doses of MMT for retention or had lower retention. 
High-dose (standard mean difference −1.17, 95% CI −1.85 to −0.49), but not medium- or low-dose, BMT decreased morphine use 
more than placebo. Medium- and flexible-dose BMT and MMT did not differ for morphine use or cocaine use. BMT vs MMT and 
high-dose BMT vs placebo did not differ for benzodiazepine use. 
Buprenorphine maintenance therapy (BMT) vs methadone maintenance therapy (MMT) or placebo for treatment retention in patients with opioid use disorder 
Comparisons *  Number of  
Trials (n)  Weighted 
Retention  
Rates  RBI (95% CI)  NNT (95% CI)  
High -dose BMT vs placebo  5 (1001)  72% vs 40%  82% (15 to 190)  4 (3 to 7)  
Medium -dose BMT vs placebo  4 (887)  66% vs 38%  74% (6 to 187)  4 (2 to 34)  
Low-dose BMT vs placebo  5 (1131)  60% vs 40%  50% (19 to 88)  5 (4 to 10)  
   RBR (95% CI)  NNH (95% CI)  
High -dose BMT vs high -dose MMT  1 (134)  5.2% vs 6.6%  21% (−216 to 20)  Not significant  
Medium -dose BMT vs medium -dose MMT  7 (780)  43% vs 48%  13% (−10 to 31)  Not significant  
Flexible -dose BMT vs flexible -dose MMT  11 (1391)  50% vs 63%  17% (5 to 27)  8 (5 to 25)  
Low-dose BMT vs low -dose MMT  3 (253)  38% vs 56%  33% (13 to 48)  6 (4 to 17)  
CI = confidence interval; NNH = number needed to harm;  NNT = number needed to treat; RBI = relative benefit increase;  
RBR = relative benefit reduction. 
*Flexible dose = dose is titrated within a broad dose range according to patient preference; low -dose BMT = 2 to 6 mg;  
medium -dose BMT = 7 to 15 mg; high -dose BMT = 16 mg; medium -dose MMT = 40 to 85 mg.  
Conclusions 
In patients with opioid use disorder, buprenorphine is more efficacious than placebo, but less efficacious than methadone therapy, for treatment retention. 
Source of funding: Australian Government Department of Health. 
Structured abstract based on: Mattick RP, Breen, C, Kimber J, Davoli M. Buprenorphine maintenance versus placebo or methadone maintenance for opioid dependence. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014;(2):CD002207. 24500948

Which of the following is most likely to bias the results of this study?

information:
- The conclusion suggests that, in patients with opioid use disorder, buprenorphine is a viable treatment option, but it is less efficacious than methadone therapy for treatment retention.
- Methadone maintenance therapy is also effective.
- The review included randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing buprenorphine maintenance therapy (BMT) with methadone maintenance therapy (MMT) and placebo.
- Studies of pregnant women and those assessing buprenorphine or methadone for detoxification without a maintenance phase were excluded.
- The outcomes considered were treatment retention and urinalysis-confirmed use of opioids, cocaine, and benzodiazepines.
- High-dose BMT did not show significant differences in treatment retention compared to high-dose MMT (RBR 21%, 95% CI −216 to 20).
- Medium-dose BMT had no significant difference in treatment retention compared to medium-dose MMT (RBR 13%, 95% CI −10 to 31).
- Buprenorphine is more efficacious than placebo for treatment retention and reducing morphine use in patients with opioid use disorder.
- It shows equivalence to methadone in some dose comparisons, though not in all.

agent_context:
The abstract displayed on this page is for use with items #17–19 on the following page. 
Question 
In patients with opioid use disorder, what is the relative efficacy of buprenorphine and methadone maintenance therapy? 
Review scope 
Included studies compared buprenorphine maintenance therapy (BMT), >1 mg/day, with methadone maintenance therapy (MMT), 
≥20 mg/day, or placebo (including 1 mg/day of buprenorphine) in patients who were dependent on heroin or other opioids. Studies of 
pregnant women and those assessing buprenorphine or methadone for detoxification but with no maintenance phase were excluded. 
Outcomes included treatment retention and urinalysis-confirmed use of morphine, cocaine, and benzodiazepines. 
Review methods 
MEDLINE and EMBASE/Excerpta Medica (2003 to Jan 2013), Cochrane Central and Cochrane Library (2013, Issue 1), PsycLIT, Current Contents, several other databases, 
ClinicalTrials.gov  and other electronic sources of ongoing trials, conference proceedings, 
Library of Congress databases, national focal points for drug research, and reference lists were searched for randomized controlled 
trials (RCTs). Authors were consulted. 31 RCTs (n=5430), ranging in size from 40 to 736 patients, met the selection criteria. Duration of study interventions ranged from 2 weeks to 52 weeks. 20 trials compared BMT with MMT, and 11 compared BMT with placebo. 
Main results 
As shown in the table below, low-, medium-, and high-dose BMT increased treatment retention more than placebo; BMT did not 
differ from equivalent doses of MMT for retention or had lower retention. 
High-dose (standard mean difference −1.17, 95% CI −1.85 to −0.49), but not medium- or low-dose, BMT decreased morphine use 
more than placebo. Medium- and flexible-dose BMT and MMT did not differ for morphine use or cocaine use. BMT vs MMT and 
high-dose BMT vs placebo did not differ for benzodiazepine use. 
Buprenorphine maintenance therapy (BMT) vs methadone maintenance therapy (MMT) or placebo for treatment retention in patients with opioid use disorder 
Comparisons *  Number of  
Trials (n)  Weighted 
Retention  
Rates  RBI (95% CI)  NNT (95% CI)  
High -dose BMT vs placebo  5 (1001)  72% vs 40%  82% (15 to 190)  4 (3 to 7)  
Medium -dose BMT vs placebo  4 (887)  66% vs 38%  74% (6 to 187)  4 (2 to 34)  
Low-dose BMT vs placebo  5 (1131)  60% vs 40%  50% (19 to 88)  5 (4 to 10)  
   RBR (95% CI)  NNH (95% CI)  
High -dose BMT vs high -dose MMT  1 (134)  5.2% vs 6.6%  21% (−216 to 20)  Not significant  
Medium -dose BMT vs medium -dose MMT  7 (780)  43% vs 48%  13% (−10 to 31)  Not significant  
Flexible -dose BMT vs flexible -dose MMT  11 (1391)  50% vs 63%  17% (5 to 27)  8 (5 to 25)  
Low-dose BMT vs low -dose MMT  3 (253)  38% vs 56%  33% (13 to 48)  6 (4 to 17)  
CI = confidence interval; NNH = number needed to harm;  NNT = number needed to treat; RBI = relative benefit increase;  
RBR = relative benefit reduction. 
*Flexible dose = dose is titrated within a broad dose range according to patient preference; low -dose BMT = 2 to 6 mg;  
medium -dose BMT = 7 to 15 mg; high -dose BMT = 16 mg; medium -dose MMT = 40 to 85 mg.  
Conclusions 
In patients with opioid use disorder, buprenorphine is more efficacious than placebo, but less efficacious than methadone therapy, for treatment retention. 
Source of funding: Australian Government Department of Health. 
Structured abstract based on: Mattick RP, Breen, C, Kimber J, Davoli M. Buprenorphine maintenance versus placebo or methadone maintenance for opioid dependence. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014;(2):CD002207. 24500948

A 38-year-old woman comes to the clinic for follow-up examination. She has opioid use disorder and currently takes methadone 
40 mg daily as maintenance therapy. She says the methadone is causing mild sedation that is interfering with her job. She would 
like to discontinue methadone maintenance therapy but is concerned about heroin relapse. She says she also takes a friend’s valium when she is feeling “stressed out.” The physician discusses alternative maintenance therapy strategies with the patient. 
The physician discusses the pros and cons of medium- or high-dose buprenorphine maintenance therapy compared with medium- 
or high-dose methadone therapy with the patient. Based on this abstract, which of the following is the most appropriate information for the physician to convey to the patient?

raw_selection_response:
<reasoning>
The clinical question is about the relative efficacy of buprenorphine (BMT) versus methadone maintenance therapy (MMT) in patients with opioid use disorder, focusing on outcomes such as treatment retention and substance use.

To answer this question given the abstract, the most clinically relevant claims are those that directly compare BMT to MMT, provide evidence on treatment retention (the main outcome endpoint), discuss morphine use as a marker of opioid misuse, and clarify that buprenorphine is less efficacious than methadone for retention but better than placebo. The abstract makes clear that while BMT is superior to placebo, it is generally less effective than MMT when it comes to treatment retention and similar or somewhat less effective in reducing morphine use except at high doses. The abstract also clarifies that there is no significant difference in benzodiazepine use between treatments, which may be relevant to the patient’s valium use but does not impact the core relative efficacy comparison.

Analyzing the answer options:
- A: This misrepresents the directionality and magnitude of the difference; the abstract states BMT is less effective than MMT, not "less by 13% and 21%", which is not how the relative benefit reduction is presented.
- B: The abstract does not show BMT to be superior for benzodiazepine use; in fact, differences are not significant.
- C: This is correct as the main conclusion.
- D: This is incorrect since both drugs are more effective than placebo.
- E: While "unclear" at some doses, the abstract specifically concludes that methadone is more effective for treatment retention, and the difference is not just "unclear".

Relevant claims:
1. **Claim 6**: States that buprenorphine is more efficacious than placebo but less than methadone for treatment retention—directly addresses the main finding.
2. **Claim 5**: States that medium and flexible-dose BMT did not differ from MMT regarding retention, morphine or cocaine use—supports that at some dosing, the effect is similar (though high-dose BMT is better than placebo for morphine use).
3. **Claim 3**: Notes that BMT (all doses) improves retention over placebo—establishes efficacy over placebo.
4. **Claim 11**: Reinforces that BMT is superior to placebo for retention.
5. (Optionally) **Claim 8**: Highlights that high-dose BMT decreases morphine use more than placebo, clarifying that at high doses, BMT can reduce illicit opioid use but not more than MMT.

The most crucial claims are #3, #5, #6, and #11 for directly addressing the efficacy comparison as framed by the abstract. Claim 8 is useful in the context of the patient’s risk for relapse but is secondary to the main treatment retention comparison.

So the most relevant claims are: **3, 5, 6, 11**.

</reasoning>
<answer>[3, 5, 6, 11]</answer>

A 27-year-old w oman comes to the office because of a 3-week history of nasal congestion, cough productive of sputum, 
intermittent moderate headache, and intermittent moderate pain over her cheeks. She has had similar symptoms three to four times yearly during the past 5 years. The symptoms last 2 to 8 weeks and generally resolve spontaneously. She has had two episodes of bacterial pneumonia during the past 5 years; both episodes resolved with antibiotic therapy. Her only current 
medication is acetaminophen as needed for headache. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. Serum study results show decreased IgA, IgG, and IgM concentrations. Antibiotic therapy is begun, and the patient’s condition improves. Which of the following is most likely to decrease the likelihood of recurrent infection in this patient?
principal_context:
A 27-year-old w oman comes to the office because of a 3-week history of nasal congestion, cough productive of sputum, 
intermittent moderate headache, and intermittent moderate pain over her cheeks. She has had similar symptoms three to four times yearly during the past 5 years. The symptoms last 2 to 8 weeks and generally resolve spontaneously. She has had two episodes of bacterial pneumonia during the past 5 years; both episodes resolved with antibiotic therapy. Her only current 
medication is acetaminophen as needed for headache. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. Serum study results show decreased IgA, IgG, and IgM concentrations. Antibiotic therapy is begun, and the patient’s condition improves. Which of the following is most likely to decrease the likelihood of recurrent infection in this patient?
information:
- The history of recurrent bacterial pneumonia indicates a possible underlying immune system defect.
- The patient has hypogammaglobulinemia (low levels of IgA, IgG, and IgM).
- Hypogammaglobulinemia is associated with sinopulmonary infections.
- Immunoglobulin Replacement Therapy (IGRT) may be beneficial in increasing immunoglobulin levels and reducing the frequency and severity of infections in hypogammaglobulinemia.
- This patient's symptoms and history suggest a likely diagnosis of chronic immunodeficiency syndrome... given the recurrent sinopulmonary infections and hypogammaglobulinemia.
- IGRT is essential to increase immunoglobulin levels and prevent sinopulmonary infections.
- Decreased immunoglobulins could predispose to repeated infections.
- Given the low immunoglobulin levels, Immunoglobulin Replacement Therapy (IRT) may be beneficial to restore normal levels and reduce susceptibility to infections.
- IRT is the most likely intervention to address the underlying susceptibility to infection given the low immunoglobulin levels.
raw_selection_response:
<reasoning>
The clinical scenario describes a young adult with recurrent sinus-pulmonary symptoms, a prior history of bacterial pneumonia, and laboratory evidence of hypogammaglobulinemia (low IgA, IgG, IgM). The prompt improvement after antibiotic therapy points to infectious episodes, and the underlying immunodeficiency is confirmed by low serum immunoglobulins.

The key clinical rationale is that hypogammaglobulinemia underlies her susceptibility to recurrent infections, especially sinopulmonary infections. Among the answer options, immune globulin replacement therapy directly addresses the root cause by replenishing antibody levels and reducing infectious complications in immunodeficient patients.

Claim relevance:
- Claims 2, 3, 4, 9, and 12 establish the presence of a likely immunodeficiency with sinopulmonary infections and low immunoglobulins.
- Claims 5, 10, 14, and 21 specifically link immunoglobulin replacement therapy (IRT/IGRT/IVIG) to reduced infection risk by restoring humoral immunity.
- Claims about prophylactic antibiotics (16, 17) discuss alternatives but are less optimal due to antibiotic resistance and immune modulation concerns.
- Claims about vaccines (20, 32) and allergy control (13, 18, 19) are tangential—they may help reduce symptoms but do not address the primary immunodeficiency mechanism.
- Claim 8 proposes a specific schedule for replacement therapy, which is supportive, but the general principle in claim 5/10/14/21 is more directly relevant to "decreasing the likelihood of recurrent infection."

Therefore, the most clinically relevant claims are those establishing the diagnosis of hypogammaglobulinemia/immunodeficiency and the role of immunoglobulin replacement therapy in reducing recurrent infections.
</reasoning>
<answer>[2, 3, 4, 5, 9, 10, 12, 14, 21]</answer>

A 17-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department because of a 1-hour history of progressive difficulty breathing and a 
2-hour history of an itchy, red rash over his trunk, arms, and legs. He has not used new soaps, detergents, or lotions during the 
past week. Thirty-six hours ago, he began a 1-week course of amoxicillin for an ear infection; he has taken four doses since that time. He has no other history of serious illness and no known allergies. He takes no other medications. Pulse is 100/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 95%. He 
appears anxious and is in moderate respiratory distress. Examination shows an erythematous, raised, demarcated rash over the trunk and all extremities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

information:
- The rash suggests a possible allergic reaction
- Amoxicillin could be implicated in the allergic reaction
- The most immediate concern is to manage the severe allergic reaction promptly
- Administer epinephrine (adrenaline) 0.01 mg/kg intramuscularly for suspected anaphylaxis
- Allergy testing may be ordered if feasible
raw_selection_response:
<reasoning>
Let's analyze the case step by step:

The patient presents with acute onset of a pruritic, erythematous, raised rash immediately after starting amoxicillin, accompanied by respiratory distress (tachypnea) and mild hypotension. The constellation of suggestive features — rash, hypotension, respiratory involvement, and temporal relationship to a drug — is highly concerning for a Type I hypersensitivity reaction, potentially progressing to anaphylaxis.

Key observations supporting an allergic reaction:
- Recent amoxicillin exposure and classic timing of drug-induced reactions
- Erythematous, raised (possibly urticarial) rash
- Respiratory involvement: tachypnea
- Mild but present hypotension
- Absence of systemic illness or triggers other than the antibiotic

SJS/TEN generally present with mucosal involvement (e.g., conjunctival, oral lesions), targetoid or bullous rash, and more severe systemic symptoms — these are not clearly present here, making them less likely.

Given the acute, potentially life-threatening nature of anaphylaxis, the priority is stabilizing the airway, breathing, and circulation. Immediate treatment should focus on:
- Recognizing and treating possible anaphylaxis (with epinephrine if indicated)
- Securing oxygenation if needed
- Rapid administration of emergency medications
- Observing for rapid clinical deterioration

Relevant claims:
1. **The rash suggests a possible allergic reaction**: The presentation is consistent with a drug allergy.
2. **Amoxicillin could be implicated in the allergic reaction**: Temporal sequence and known allergen make this likely.
3. **The most immediate concern is to manage the severe allergic reaction promptly**: Anaphylaxis can deteriorate rapidly.
4. **Call for immediate assistance for possible urgent transfer to a critical care setting**: High risk for progression.
5. **Secure the patient's airway and administer supplemental oxygen**: Pulse ox is 95%, hypotension and respiratory distress.
6. **Discontinue the amoxicillin immediately**: Essential in suspected allergic reaction.
7. **Administer epinephrine (adrenaline) 0.01 mg/kg IM for suspected anaphylaxis**: First-line therapy for anaphylaxis.
8. **Hypotension and tachypnea indicate distress**: Supports need for urgent action.
9. **Observe for signs of anaphylaxis... and manage promptly with epinephrine if indicated**: Matches clinical signs.
10. **Administration of oxygen and monitoring vital signs is essential**: Because oxygenation and hemodynamics are compromised.
11. **For an allergic reaction, administer antihistamines and corticosteroids**: Should be done after or alongside epinephrine, but not first-line emergency treatment.
12. **SJS/TEN are less likely without severe mucosal/systemic involvement**: Helps rule out but not the most urgent consideration.
13. **Other differentials exist but are less likely**.

Given the acute risk of anaphylaxis, the top priority is to secure the airway, give oxygen if needed, and give IM epinephrine immediately. That makes claims 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 most clinically relevant.

However, the question asks for the most appropriate *next step*, and the answer options are:
A. Albuterol
B. Diphenhydramine
C. Epinephrine
D. CBC
E. Observation

Clearly, the most relevant claims support immediate administration of epinephrine (claim 7/30) as the first-line management for potential anaphylaxis.

Thus, the single most clinically relevant claim to inform the next step is **"Administer epinephrine (adrenaline) 0.01 mg/kg intramuscularly for suspected anaphylaxis"** (claim 8), and closely related are claims about recognizing and managing anaphylaxis (1, 2, 4, 9, 10).

But as instructed to pick only the most directly relevant to the *next step*, the absolute priorities are:

- **Claim 8**: "Administer epinephrine (adrenaline) 0.01 mg/kg intramuscularly for suspected anaphylaxis" (directly dictates the next step)
- **Claim 1**: The rash suggestive of allergy
- **Claim 2**: Amoxicillin implicated
- **Claim 4**: Manage the allergic reaction promptly
- **Claim 9/20**: Anaphylaxis should be managed with epinephrine
- **Claim 5**: Call for rapid help if severe
- **Claim 6**: Discontinue the drug

But since the next step must be chosen immediately, and the answer is clearly **C. Administration of epinephrine**, the most critical claim is #8.

I will select the most precise and actionable relevant claims:
**1, 2, 4, 8, 9**

But if strict to the absolute directly actionable claim for the next immediate step, just #8 suffices. However, to demonstrate a well-justified clinical thought process, I will include the set above.

Final selection: claims 1, 2, 4, 8, 9

</reasoning>
<answer>[1, 2, 4, 8, 9]</answer>

A 57-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance 30 minutes after he had loss of consciousness. His wife 
says he was making dinner when he fell on the kitchen floor. He has hypertension and his only medication is lisinopril. He has no history of operative procedures. En route, his temperature was 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse was 90/min, respirations were 10/min and labored, and blood pressure was 180/100 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 40% oxygen by face mask showed an oxygen saturation 
of 99%. On arrival, he is unresponsive. His Glasgow Coma Scale score is 3. He is intubated and mechanically ventilated. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 90/min, ventilatory rate is 10/min, and blood pressure is 180/100 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on an F
IO2 of 0.4 shows an oxygen saturation of 98%. On examination, Doll's eye (oculocephalic) maneuver shows absent eye 
movements, and the corneal reflex is absent. On ice-water caloric testing, there is no nystagmus or deviation of the eyes toward the ear being irrigated. CT scan of the head shows a ruptured cerebral aneurysm. When the patient's wife is notified of the patient's condition, she says he wanted to be an organ donor but does not have an advance directive. Which of the following is the most 
appropriate next step in management?

raw_selection_response:
<reasoning>
The patient presents with a catastrophic neurological injury likely due to a ruptured cerebral aneurysm, currently in a coma with signs of brainstem herniation. Immediate clinical priorities are neurological and hemodynamic stabilization, neurosurgical intervention, and ethical/legal considerations regarding organ donation, as the patient's family has raised the topic. 

Relevant claims include those outlining the clinical scenario (findings, neurological status, CT evidence of ruptured aneurysm), the need for immediate neurosurgical consultation (since operative intervention is the standard for ruptured aneurysm in a hemodynamically unstable patient), management of hypertension and mechanical ventilation, and the crucial step regarding organ donation when the patient has expressed a wish for donation. However, organ donation procedures cannot proceed arbitrarily: the next actual clinical step before contacting organ procurement must be to ensure the legal and ethical process is followed, which critically includes consulting the hospital’s ethics committee when consent or advance directives are unclear or insufficient.

Therefore, the most relevant claims are those that establish the acute condition and need for neurosurgical intervention, that confirm the patient is not brain dead and active treatment is ongoing, and that highlight the need to follow the proper ethical and legal steps regarding organ donation consent in the absence of a formal advance directive. 

Specifically:
- Claims regarding neurological assessment and diagnosis (1, 15, 24, 26, 27)
- Need for neurosurgical input (5, 10, 19)
- Blood pressure and ventilatory management (3, 4, 17, 20, 33)
- The need for a formal ethical and legal process before proceeding with organ donation (1, 14, 31, and most critically, 6 is about donor management but 31 and the scenario highlight the necessity of proper consent process)

However, given the question’s focus on the "most appropriate next step," the key is Claim 1 (brain herniation) to clarify the urgency and gravity, and Claim 14 or 31—both emphasizing the necessity of consent and following guidelines with next of kin and possibly the ethics committee. Claim 31 highlights the need to initiate the conversation with the family and follow local protocols. But the most accurate and legally safer next step in this scenario (where the wife says there is no advance directive but the patient wished to be a donor) is to ensure ethical review, as per clinical standards. Thus, Claim 14 (laying out options with family and consenting process) and Claim 31 (initiating discussion and following protocols) are crucial, but the **direct next appropriate clinical step** is to consult hospital ethics when consent is unclear or complex, as per best practice guidelines and the options provided (Answer Option A).

Therefore, the most clinically relevant claims are:
- 1 (brain herniation: urgency and gravity)
- 5 or 10 (neurosurgical consultation)
- 14 (consenting, ethical/protocol process)
- 31 (initiating organ donation process, but only after consent and ethics are addressed)

But since the most directly correct next step is an ethics consultation (A), the most relevant supporting claims are:
- Claim 1 (to indicate severity/need for timely action)

information:

- Inflammatory edema results from leakage of plasma and proteins into interstitial spaces due to compromised vascular integrity.
- The most likely diagnosis is an abscess.
raw_selection_response:

To determine the primary mechanism of edema in this patient, it's critical first to identify the likely diagnosis based on the clinical scenario. The patient has pain, swelling, erythema, tenderness, and a fluctuant lesion at a site of recent trauma, along with fever. This constellation of findings strongly suggests an infectious process, specifically an abscess (Claim 9, 22, 29). 

Among the options for the mechanism of edema, the most plausible one in infectious inflammation is the result of inflammatory response causing vascular permeability and movement of fluid into the tissues. This is supported by claims that explain the infectious/inflammatory etiology (Claims 12, 25). Cellulitis and abscesses cause local inflammation, which increases vascular permeability, leading to the leakage of plasma and proteins into the interstitial space — creating edema. 

Let's review each answer choice with the relevant claims:
- A. Degranulation of eosinophils: Unlikely, since this suggests an allergic or eosinophilic process (as noted in Claim 10), which is less likely here.
- B. Disruption of vascular basement membranes: More relevant to hemorrhage or chronic vasculitis, not typical for acute abscess/cellulitis.
- C. Increased hydrostatic pressure: Unlikely in this localized, acute scenario (Claim 11, 28).
- D. Release of thromboxane: While inflammatory mediators are released, thromboxane's role is more related to platelet aggregation and vasoconstriction, not the primary edema mechanism here.
- E. Separation of endothelial junctions: This directly corresponds to increased vascular permeability due to inflammation (Claim 25). During inflammation (as in abscess/cellulitis), cytokines and mediators cause endothelial junction disruption, leading to plasma leakage and edema.

The relevant claims include those that:
- Confirm an infectious/inflammatory diagnosis (Claims 1, 3, 5, 9, 22, 29)
- Explain how inflammation leads to edema (Claims 12, 25)
- Highlight the nature of the lesion (abscess with fluctuance) and necessity of drainage (Claims 7, 30)
- Ruled out other less likely causes (Claims 10, 11, 28)

Therefore, the most clinically relevant claims regarding the mechanism of edema are those that identify the diagnosis as infection/inflammation and state that edema results from compromised endothelial integrity (increased vascular permeability):

Claims 12 ("The primary mechanism of edema is infection and inflammation, involving increased blood flow and migration of white blood cells.") and 25 ("Inflammatory edema results from leakage of plasma and proteins into interstitial spaces due to compromised vascular integrity.") directly address this mechanism and support Answer Option E.

Additionally, Claims 22 ("A fluctuant erythematous lesion is suggestive of an abscess or pus collection.") and 29 ("The most likely diagnosis is an abscess.") are essential to confirming the diagnosis that underlies the mechanism.

However, since the question specifically asks for the mechanism of edema, not the diagnosis, the primary claims supporting the correct mechanism are 12 and 25. But to comprehensively justify choosing Option E (separation of endothelial junctions), which refers to increased vascular permeability due to inflammation, these claims are most relevant.

Final selection should include claims that confirm the diagnosis (abscess/infection), explain how inflammation causes edema (endothelial separation/increased permeability), and rule out other mechanisms.
</reasoning>
<answer>[12, 22, 25, 29]</answer>

lenses for reading. He has not had pain in his eye or photophobia. He has no history of trauma to the area. On examination, visual

through closed eyelids shows no abnormalities. There is normal red reflex bilaterally. The lenses appear clear. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

- The AAO recommends an ophthalmic evaluation for patients with unilateral or bilateral visual disturbances, particularly if there is no clear cause.
- A referral to an ophthalmologist is warranted for a comprehensive eye examination.
- The ophthalmologist should perform a comprehensive eye exam, including visual acuity testing, refraction, and retinoscopy, to exclude refractive error.
- Further investigation may include OCT or optical coherence tomography to evaluate the structure of the retina.
- Macular metamorphopsia (perceived distortion of straight lines) is a key feature of Age-related Macular Degeneration.
- AMD is a common cause of progressive, central visual loss in the elderly.

The patient is a 72-year-old man with a 3-week history of visual distortion—curved lines and wavy blinds—which is most characteristic of metamorphopsia, a hallmark symptom of macular degeneration. Other symptoms commonly associated with serious eye disease, such as pain, photophobia, acute vision loss, or trauma, are absent. The visual acuity is reduced in the right eye but not profoundly decreased in the left, and both pupils are normal, ruling out acute optic neuritis or anterior ischemic optic neuropathy. The clear lenses and normal red reflex on exam make cataracts less likely, as cataracts typically cause clouding and a diminished red reflex. Glaucoma usually presents with peripheral vision loss rather than central distortion, and retinal detachment or vascular occlusions are less likely given the absence of flashes, floaters, or acute vision loss.

The patient's age and bilateral mild reduction in acuity, along with the symptom of metamorphopsia, are highly suggestive of age-related macular degeneration (AMD), especially given the chronicity. The preserved red reflex and absence of elevated intraocular pressure arguments also decrease the likelihood of glaucoma or cataract. Given the potential for AMD to cause irreversible central vision loss and the availability of imaging and treatment options such as OCT and anti-VEGF therapy, urgent referral to an ophthalmologist and specific AMD-focused evaluation are warranted.

agent_context:
The abstract displayed on this page is for use with items #17–19 on the following page. 
Question 
In patients with opioid use disorder, what is the relative efficacy of buprenorphine and methadone maintenance therapy? 
Review scope 
Included studies compared buprenorphine maintenance therapy (BMT), >1 mg/day, with methadone maintenance therapy (MMT), 
≥20 mg/day, or placebo (including 1 mg/day of buprenorphine) in patients who were dependent on heroin or other opioids. Studies of 
pregnant women and those assessing buprenorphine or methadone for detoxification but with no maintenance phase were excluded. 
Outcomes included treatment retention and urinalysis-confirmed use of morphine, cocaine, and benzodiazepines. 
Review methods 
MEDLINE and EMBASE/Excerpta Medica (2003 to Jan 2013), Cochrane Central and Cochrane Library (2013, Issue 1), PsycLIT, Current Contents, several other databases, 
ClinicalTrials.gov  and other electronic sources of ongoing trials, conference proceedings, 
Library of Congress databases, national focal points for drug research, and reference lists were searched for randomized controlled 
trials (RCTs). Authors were consulted. 31 RCTs (n=5430), ranging in size from 40 to 736 patients, met the selection criteria. Duration of study interventions ranged from 2 weeks to 52 weeks. 20 trials compared BMT with MMT, and 11 compared BMT with placebo. 
Main results 
As shown in the table below, low-, medium-, and high-dose BMT increased treatment retention more than placebo; BMT did not 
differ from equivalent doses of MMT for retention or had lower retention. 
High-dose (standard mean difference −1.17, 95% CI −1.85 to −0.49), but not medium- or low-dose, BMT decreased morphine use 
more than placebo. Medium- and flexible-dose BMT and MMT did not differ for morphine use or cocaine use. BMT vs MMT and 
high-dose BMT vs placebo did not differ for benzodiazepine use. 
Buprenorphine maintenance therapy (BMT) vs methadone maintenance therapy (MMT) or placebo for treatment retention in patients with opioid use disorder 
Comparisons *  Number of  
Trials (n)  Weighted 
Retention  
Rates  RBI (95% CI)  NNT (95% CI)  
High -dose BMT vs placebo  5 (1001)  72% vs 40%  82% (15 to 190)  4 (3 to 7)  
Medium -dose BMT vs placebo  4 (887)  66% vs 38%  74% (6 to 187)  4 (2 to 34)  
Low-dose BMT vs placebo  5 (1131)  60% vs 40%  50% (19 to 88)  5 (4 to 10)  
   RBR (95% CI)  NNH (95% CI)  
High -dose BMT vs high -dose MMT  1 (134)  5.2% vs 6.6%  21% (−216 to 20)  Not significant  
Medium -dose BMT vs medium -dose MMT  7 (780)  43% vs 48%  13% (−10 to 31)  Not significant  
Flexible -dose BMT vs flexible -dose MMT  11 (1391)  50% vs 63%  17% (5 to 27)  8 (5 to 25)  
Low-dose BMT vs low -dose MMT  3 (253)  38% vs 56%  33% (13 to 48)  6 (4 to 17)  
CI = confidence interval; NNH = number needed to harm;  NNT = number needed to treat; RBI = relative benefit increase;  
RBR = relative benefit reduction. 
*Flexible dose = dose is titrated within a broad dose range according to patient preference; low -dose BMT = 2 to 6 mg;  
medium -dose BMT = 7 to 15 mg; high -dose BMT = 16 mg; medium -dose MMT = 40 to 85 mg.  
Conclusions 
In patients with opioid use disorder, buprenorphine is more efficacious than placebo, but less efficacious than methadone therapy, for treatment retention. 
Source of funding: Australian Government Department of Health. 
Structured abstract based on: Mattick RP, Breen, C, Kimber J, Davoli M. Buprenorphine maintenance versus placebo or methadone maintenance for opioid dependence. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014;(2):CD002207. 24500948
